But there were too many of them for the JLC to represent individually, and certainly not in Shavarela's court. No, for a case this size and this serious, they'd have to file a petition with the state Supreme Court. The problem was, the JLC had no idea who or where these 600 kids were. Their records had been sealed, so their names were hidden from the public. And the JLC couldn't depend on the courts to help find them.

January 2009, early January 2009, we get a one-sentence order from the Pennsylvania Supreme Court denying this petition. That was probably one of the lowest points in my career. The explanation they received was essentially, you claim that hundreds of kids are affected by this, but only a handful of them are named. So as far as we're concerned, this isn't significant enough to warrant our intervention. That shook my confidence in the legal system. It was a tough blow for all of them, but maybe especially for Hillary. Ever since I came back from jail, I was like, Shivarella is going down. And teachers literally laughed at me. They said, Shivarella will never go to jail. They were like, you're an idiot. Basically, they said, do you know how long he's been judged? How long he's been doing this? Do you know how well connected he is? Nothing's going to happen to him. You're an idiot. And I just, I was like, no, you don't know. There's something more going on and you're wrong.
